turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The second opinion for the court that I have is No. 21-1450, Turkey Halkbank v. United States. Halkbank is a bank owned by the Republic of Turkey. In 2019, the United States indicted Halkbank in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York. According to the indictment, Halkbank with the assistance of high-ranking Turkish government officials laundered billions of dollars of Iranian oil and gas proceeds through the Global Financial System, including the U.S. Financial System in violation of U.S. Sanctions and numerous federal laws. Two individual defendants, including a former Halkbank executive have already been convicted in Federal Court for their roles in the conspiracy. According to the U.S. government, several other indicted defendants, including Halkbank's former general manager and its former head of foreign operations remain at large. Before trial, Halkbank raised several arguments that the indictment should be dismissed. Both the District Court and the Second Circuit Court of Appeals rejected those arguments. Given the importance of the issues and the involvement of an instrumentality of a foreign nation as a defendant in the U.S. Criminal justice system, this court granted review to consider the issues before any trial occurs. We now reject two of Halkbank's arguments and leave the third for further consideration by the lower courts on remand. First, Halkbank argues that the General Federal Criminal Jurisdiction Statute Section 3231 of Title 18 does not extend the prosecution's of instrumentalities of foreign states such as Halkbank. We disagree with Halkbank. Section 3231 opens Federal District Courts to the full range of federal prosecutions for violations of federal criminal law. By its term, Section 3231 plainly encompasses Halkbank's alleged criminal offenses. Second, Halkbank alternatively argues that the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of 1976 provides instrumentalities of foreign states with absolute immunity from criminal prosecution in U.S. Courts. We again disagree with Halkbank. That act creates a comprehensive scheme governing claims of immunity against foreign states and their instrumentalities, but as the statute's text and structure indicate, the act applies only in civil cases, not in criminal cases like this one. In arguing that we should nonetheless apply the act to criminal cases, Halkbank suggests that U.S. criminal proceedings against instrumentalities of foreign states would negatively affect U.S. national security and foreign policy. But it is not our role as judges to rewrite the act based on purported foreign policy concerns that Congress and the president have not seen fit to recognize. In short, the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act does not provide Halkbank with immunity from criminal proceedings. Third, Halkbank advances one other plea for immunity. This Court has recognized that a suit not governed by the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act may still be barred by foreign sovereign immunity under the common law. The Court of Appeals did not fully consider the arguments regarding common law immunity that the parties press in this Court. We express no view on those issues and leave them for consideration on remand. In sum, we hold that the District Court has jurisdiction over the prosecution of Halkbank and that the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act does not grant immunity from criminal prosecution to Halkbank. We affirm the Court of Appeals on those two issues. With respect to common law immunity, we vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals and remand. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part, which Justice Alito has joined.
